polls.text: Is the problem space sufficiently well understood?
Is there a problem worth solving?
Is the IETF the right place to solve this problem?
Is this proposed charter the right basis to form an IETF working group?
Do you plan to help discuss, write, review, implement, or deploy 🥔?
Would you actively participate in PTTH?

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: mt: I-D draft names don't directly support Emoji, so obviously the solution is Punycode.  May I suggest naming your draft draft-thomson-ptth-xn--qr9h?

chat.author: altanai
chat.text: the ethernet connection is tuber-charged :)

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: Do we understand well what the current limitations are of directionality in HTTP?

chat.author: altanai
chat.text: is same-transport bidirectional request initiation a hard requirement, or would separate forward and reverse HTTP connections be acceptable?

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: Mirja: only clients can send requests in standard HTTP.  This is limiting in the use-cases presented.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: https://github.com/ietf-wg-ptth/charter/issues/3 added to track the basic issue I raised.

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: @Altanai Surely the forward and reverse connections can always be forcibly separated by placing them on different origins/hostnames?  Maybe the interesting question is whether there are situations where you need the connections to be separate but you also need the "proxy" to have the same identity (i.e. hostname) for both purposes.

chat.author: Rahul Gupta
chat.text: Heavy echo from the room.

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: audio is also breaking up remote

chat.author: Michael Toomim
chat.text: Are you still seeing echoes out there? They are probably causing the audio drops.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Perhaps the remote audio is too loud and is being picked up by the in-room mics?

chat.author: Rahul Gupta
chat.text: Remote drops are increasing in frequency. All monosyllabic. Can @meetecho please look at it

chat.author: Michael Toomim
chat.text: When the in-room mics pick up audio, they turn off the presenter's audio.

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: There's not much we can do if we get broken audio from the source: we'd normally suggest dropping video to prioritize audio, but I see the speaker was audio-only already

chat.author: Jan-Frederik Rieckers
chat.text: My theory, because I didn't see the presenter using headphones: The echo from the room caused echo-compensation on the presenter's side.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: There's also a need for an overloaded proxy to request that workers connect not to it but to its load-balanced or failover sibling

chat.author: altanai
chat.text: @Benjamin Schwartz my case was : can I keep one configured proxy identity while still requiring distinct forward and reverse connections for role clarity, authorization, load isolation ... or failure isloation which is kinda useful for ZTNA  usecases where ops demand wants separate control/data  forward/reverse connections.

chat.author: Michael Toomim
chat.text: These seem to be going beyond "clarifying questions" -- and are talking about solutions.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: We could issue RFC3040bis!  (https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3040, which assigned many names which never really caught on)

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: We should come up with potato-related terminology for all the entities.  It should be a goal of this WG to have as many potato-related puns as PLANTS has plant-related ones.

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: maybe expand to include other nightshades, not just the potato

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text: 301 Permanently Mashed

chat.author: Yaroslav Rosomakho
chat.text: And establish liaison relationships with World Potato Congress, Potato Expo, PotatoEurope and other influential relevant SDOs

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Michael Toomim I think that we should firmly put those ideas out of scope.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Ultimately, this is not at the application layer, but really about the plumbing.

chat.author: Yaroslav Rosomakho
chat.text: I believe that for browser centric use cases we already have a number of reasonably successful methods to subscribe to events or make application-specific server to client requests. But they don't really scale well for CDN origins or ZTNAs

chat.author: Marius Kleidl
chat.text: The mentioned use case of pushing updates and events seems to be similar to what HTTP/2's server push was.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Marius and we all remember how well that went.

chat.author: Michael Toomim
chat.text: @Martin Thomson I think the first one should be in scope: Two servers connecting to each other over a single TCP connection.

chat.author: Michael Toomim
chat.text: What we need is bidirectoinality.

chat.author: Michael Toomim
chat.text: Rather than two reversed connections.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: https://github.com/ietf-wg-ptth/charter/blob/main/charter.md

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: could we not have the weird backronym? just say "it's ptth, like http backwards"

chat.author: Michael P
chat.text: Nit on scope: "produce a standards-track specification that defines:..." implies that all this will be in one document. We might not want to be bound to that

chat.author: Kazuho Oku
chat.text: wonder if we it makes sense to go to the next para (out of scope) before discussing

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: @Martin Thomson why did you cross out Authentication in your slide for problem 2?

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: As Colin just pointed out in MoQ, having that guidance be in a separated document that can be revised without revising the protocol document can be valuable.

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: +1 Mark

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text:
Alan Frindell said:

Mirja: only clients can send requests in standard HTTP.  This is limiting in the use-cases presented.

Yes, that is how the specs are written but I mean do we know what would need to change to lift that? Where are the break points?

chat.author: Dragana Damjanovic
chat.text: +1 Mark

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Is working out what the same origin policy means here already in scope?  If so, which bullet?

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: I guess "origin constraints" covers it, sorry.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I think fractional origins should be something that's neither in nor out of scope, but rather something that the WG can decide.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Either in the core protocol or for a future extension

chat.author: Kazuho Oku
chat.text: yeah reverse proxies allows forwarding a fraction of origin to a backend; excluding that from scope limits the capability of PTTH to a small subset of HTTP

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Marco Munizaga said:

Martin Thomson why did you cross out Authentication in your slide for problem 2?

Oops, not watching chat, writing code.  I think that it isn't really the goal of the slide.  Authorization is the goal, authentication is (sometimes) the way you get there.

chat.author: Mike Bishop
chat.text: The WG will not define reverse-NFSv4.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Michael Toomim you mention wanting to put requests flowing in both directions over the same connection.  That's really, really hard in HTTP/{2|3} without tunneling.  With tunneling, it's easy.  Tunneling (masque etc...) lets us solve the problem simply, but if you force the design to accommodate both in the same envelope, you are asking for a world of pain.

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: Basically, I think there's an attractive-looking option here of "Just do TLS client auth using a WebPKI server certificate", and I'm trying to figure out what to say about that.

chat.author: Etienne Dechamps
chat.text: Martin: I can think of trivial ways to have requests flowing in both ways in H2 or H3. For example, the worker/origin could send long-lived GET requests, and when the gateway/proxy has a request t send to the worker, it can reply to that long-lived GET request with an HTTP request (so, an HTTP request in response to an HTTP request). On the same connection, it is trivial to have normal HTTP requests flying over the same connection at the same time.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Ben: do we need to say anything about it in the charter, as opposed to in the working group when it's deciding on its design?

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I'm generally opposed to making too many design decisions in the charter

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Etienne Dechamps you don't get all of HTTP that way, so trivial solutions trivialize the results...

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: So I think for round one, I think those would all be out of scope.  Once it exists, maybe.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I've taken a swing at trying to make charter amendments here: https://github.com/ietf-wg-ptth/charter/pull/8

chat.author: Rahul Gupta
chat.text: Wait while we review @mt pr

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: How many people in the room plan to do the work

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Rahul Gupta Sorry for the distraction, I really only wanted to ensure that the discussion was captured.  Don't insist on the PR being complete and accurate.

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: the potato emoji renders on the remote participant view :) :potato:

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I think "no opinion" means 'don't know yet"

chat.author: Max Gendler
chat.text: 'no opinion' means you can maybe talk us into it

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Meetecho: emoji aren't displaying properly in the on-screen pool

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: This is probably an issue with the installed font packages on the meetecho machines in the room.  Not a big deal that the :potato: doesn't render.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Low-priority issue for Meetecho's issue tracker

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Obviously, we need up-to-date emoji support for all future protocol development

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: Do you mean in the slides? I think I do see the potato in the chat

chat.author: altanai
chat.text: I would be willing to participate "actively" to this from the ZTNA/SDWAN perspective , provided we make that a usecase ofcourse :fries:

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox

chat.text: Sorry, wasn't intentional!

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Fewer raw potatoes, more tasty potato dishes
